methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $50M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 1 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Fuse Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Nuclear Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M+

data.narrative:
Elementl Power is a Series A nuclear energy company that has raised $50M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws from a peer universe of 11 total comparables—3 public (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and 8 private firms including Fuse Energy, TerraPower, X-Energy, and Kairos Power—though the private peer set lacks sufficient same-stage or valuation-tier overlap for reliable median benchmarking.

The stage-progression method is the primary applicable approach here. Using total capital raised ($50M) as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical for Series A-to-next-stage advancement yields an implied valuation of $100M. This method is grounded in observed market patterns for nuclear technology companies advancing through growth stages, though it remains sensitive to capital efficiency and technology de-risking realized between rounds.

The peer-median method is not applicable due to insufficient same-stage or comparable-tier comparables in the nuclear energy sector; leveraging all-sector medians would obscure stage and market-specific dynamics. The analysis therefore triangulates to a range of $85M (low) to $115M (high), with a median of $100M. Key uncertainties center on regulatory progress, technology validation milestones achieved since the $50M raise, and relative capital efficiency versus the broader nuclear frontier-tech cohort. Additional same-stage peer data or marked technical de-risking would be necessary to narrow this range materially.
